title: Differentials vs Differences
summary: The distinction between dx and delta x.
author: Dr. Aidin Jalilzadeh
body:
# Differentials in Calculus: the story of $\tiny{dx}$ vs $\tiny{\delta x}$.

When you're evaluating an integral, say finding the area under some function $f(x)$ from $x=a$ to $x=b$. The first thing you write is the integral expression with the usual notations and conventions - something like: $\int\_{a}^{b} f(x)dx$. By instinct you (and I too) know that any integral expression needs a $dx$ or in physics and mechanics $dt$ since almost all quantities are a function of time.

title: A-Level Math vs AP Math vs IB Math: What Is the Difference?
summary: My take on the differences between the three major curricula.
author: Dr. Aidin Jalilzadeh
body:
If you are trying to choose between A-level Math, AP Math, and IB Math, the confusing part is that all three can be academically strong, but they are built around very different ideas of what mathematics education should look like.

At first glance, they may seem similar. Students study algebra, functions, calculus, statistics, and problem solving in all three systems. But the experience of learning them can feel very different. One course may push for deep procedural fluency, another may emphasize college-style unit exams, and another may connect mathematics to modeling, interpretation, and written communication.

The biggest difference is not just *what* students learn. It is *how* they are expected to think.

## The simplest way to think about it

A-level Math is usually the most focused and traditional in style. It tends to reward strong algebraic technique, careful working, and comfort with formal exam questions.

AP Math is the most U.S. college-aligned in structure. It is designed around standardized advanced high school courses such as AP Calculus AB, AP Calculus BC, and AP Statistics, each with its own exam and scope.

IB Math is the most curriculum-driven and philosophy-driven. It is designed not only to teach math content, but also to develop a particular kind of mathematical thinker. Depending on the course, that may mean emphasis on proof and abstraction or on real-world modeling and interpretation.

## A-level Math: depth through a traditional academic route

A-level Math is common in the UK and in many international schools following a British-style curriculum. Students usually specialize more than they would in a broad diploma system, so mathematics is taken as a serious subject rather than as one part of a wide general package.

A-level Math tends to feel clean and direct. Students learn methods, practice them heavily, and apply them to increasingly difficult problems. Algebra matters a lot. So does being able to manipulate expressions quickly and accurately. A student might solve equations, analyze graphs, work with trigonometric identities, differentiate and integrate functions, and answer mechanics or statistics questions depending on the specification.

A typical A-level style question often expects students to move fluently through symbolic steps. For example, a student may need to solve something like ${2x^2 - 5x - 3 = 0}$ quickly and accurately, or differentiate a function such as ${y = x^3 \ln x}$ using product rules and logarithmic understanding.

The strength of A-level Math is that it can build very solid mathematical technique. Students who enjoy structure, repetition, and precision often do very well in it. It can be especially strong preparation for university subjects that require algebraic confidence, including engineering, physics, economics, and mathematics itself.

The tradeoff is that it can feel narrower. It is often less interested in reflective writing, less interested in extended investigations, and less interested in interdisciplinary connections than IB. It is also not broken into separate branded courses in the same way AP is. It is more like committing to a rigorous subject pathway.

## AP Math: college-style courses with clear boundaries

AP Math is a little different because it is not one course. When people say “AP Math,” they usually mean one or more of the following: AP Calculus AB, AP Calculus BC, or AP Statistics. Each one is its own course with its own exam.

That means AP Math is modular. A student might take AP Calculus AB without taking AP Statistics. Another student might take AP Statistics but not calculus. A stronger math student may take BC, which goes further than AB.

This structure makes AP flexible. Schools and students can choose the course that fits their goals. A student heading toward engineering may prioritize AP Calculus BC. A student interested in psychology, biology, business, or social science may benefit greatly from AP Statistics.

AP Calculus is usually fast-paced and focused on core college calculus topics: limits, derivatives, integrals, accumulation, applications, and in BC, additional material such as sequences, series, parametric equations, and polar functions. Students learn both procedures and interpretation, but the course is tightly shaped by the exam.

For example, in AP Calculus, a student may study a derivative like

$$
\frac{d}{dx}(x^2 \sin x) = 2x \sin x + x^2 \cos x
$$

but they are also expected to explain what that derivative means in context, such as a rate of change or slope of a tangent line.

AP Statistics has a very different personality. It is less about algebraic elegance and more about data, variability, inference, sampling, probability, and conclusions. A student may calculate a test statistic, interpret a confidence interval, or explain whether a study supports causation.

The main strength of AP Math is clarity. Each course has a defined identity. If a student wants calculus, they take calculus. If they want statistics, they take statistics. It is a very practical system.

The downside is that AP can sometimes feel fragmented. It does not always create the same unified mathematical identity that A-level or IB can create. Also, because AP is course-specific, the overall experience depends a lot on which AP math course a student actually takes.

## IB Math: one subject, different philosophies

IB Math stands apart because it is not just about content coverage. It is about the kind of mathematical thinking the course is trying to develop.

The modern IB system typically divides students into pathways such as Analysis and Approaches and Applications and Interpretation, each available at different levels. Broadly speaking, one pathway leans more toward algebraic structure, proof, and symbolic reasoning, while the other leans more toward modeling, statistics, technology, and real-world use of mathematics.

That distinction matters.

A student in a more analysis-focused route may spend more time with formal functions, calculus, and abstract reasoning. A student in a more applications-focused route may spend more time interpreting graphs, building models, and using mathematics to describe practical situations.

For example, IB-style mathematics is often comfortable asking students not only to solve an equation, but also to explain what the result means in context. A student might model population growth with an exponential function such as $P(t)=P_0 e^{kt}$, and then discuss what the parameter ${k}$ represents, whether the model is realistic over a long period, and what assumptions are built into it.

That is very IB. The mathematics is not separated from interpretation.

Another major feature of IB Math is the internal assessment. Students usually complete an exploration, which is an extended piece of mathematical writing. This means IB students are not only solving timed exam questions. They are also expected to investigate a topic, communicate mathematics clearly, and present reasoning in a structured way.

This makes IB Math feel broader and sometimes more demanding in a different sense. It is not always the hardest in pure symbolic manipulation, depending on the course and level, but it often asks students to be more versatile. They need to calculate, interpret, communicate, and reflect.

## Which one is hardest?

There is no single honest answer because “hardest” depends on the student.

A student who is very strong algebraically and likes traditional exams may find A-level Math more natural than IB. A student who likes clear exam preparation and focused units may thrive in AP. A student who is comfortable writing, interpreting, and connecting ideas may perform especially well in IB.

Difficulty also depends on the exact course. AP Calculus BC is not the same experience as AP Statistics. IB Math AA HL is not the same as IB Math AI SL. A-level Math is not the same as Further Mathematics.

So the better question is not “Which is hardest?” but “Which kind of difficulty suits the student best?”

## Which one prepares students best?

Again, it depends on the next step.

For students heading into mathematically intensive university programs, A-level Math can offer excellent technical preparation, especially when paired with Further Mathematics. AP Calculus BC can also provide strong preparation, especially in U.S.-style pathways. IB Math, particularly the more rigorous pathways and levels, can prepare students well too, but often with the added benefit of broader mathematical communication and modeling experience.

For students who want flexibility, AP is often attractive because they can choose specific subjects. For students who want a coherent diploma program with mathematics embedded in a bigger academic framework, IB makes more sense. For students who want specialization and traditional academic rigor, A-level is often the strongest fit.

## Final thoughts

A-level Math, AP Math, and IB Math are not just three versions of the same course. They reflect three different educational philosophies.

A-level Math says: master the mathematics with precision and depth.

AP Math says: take advanced subject-based courses that mirror college expectations.

IB Math says: learn mathematics as a language for reasoning, modeling, and communicating ideas.

None of these is automatically better than the others. The best choice depends on the student’s strengths, future plans, and preferred way of learning.

The right question is not which curriculum has the biggest name. The right question is which one helps a student become the strongest mathematician they can be.
